steps.title: Compare the leave groups
steps.content: The conclusion says longer leave might not give a greater cognitive benefit than shorter leave. To support that claim, the graph must directly compare the two leave groups after the leave period: staff who took 2-4 days and staff who took 1-5 weeks. A comparison with the no-leave group, or with scores from before anyone took leave, does not address whether longer leave produced a bigger cognitive gain than shorter leave.

steps.title: Use the post-leave scores
steps.content: Choice A mixes the short-leave group with the no-leave group and then compares no leave with long leave, so it never tests the key relationship. Choice B uses the first test administration, which occurred before leave and cannot show the effect of taking leave. Choice D compares short leave with no leave, not short leave with long leave. The remaining choice uses the second and third test administrations and shows the 2-4 day group scoring higher than the 1-5 week group both times, supporting the idea that longer leave did not confer a greater benefit. The answer is C.
